refashioned:
In the first part of this chapter, I tried to show, even on the mercantilist system's own principles, how unnecessary it is to impose extraordinary restrictions on imports from countries with which the trade balance is supposedly unfavorable.

Nothing, however, can be more absurd than this entire doctrine of the balance of trade, on which not only these restrictions but almost all other commercial regulations are founded. The doctrine assumes that when two countries trade, if the balance is even, neither gains nor loses; but if it tilts to one side, one gains and the other loses in proportion. Both assumptions are false. A trade that is forced through bounties and monopolies may be, and usually is, disadvantageous to the country it is meant to favor, as I will show later. But trade that is carried on naturally and freely between any two places is always advantageous, though not always equally so, to both.
elegant: Having shown that balance-of-trade restrictions are unnecessary even on mercantilist principles, Smith now rejects the entire doctrine. Its core assumptions -- that even trade means no gain, and uneven trade means one side loses -- are both false. Forced trade through bounties and monopolies usually harms the favored country. But natural, free trade between any two places is always beneficial to both, though not always equally.
summary: The balance-of-trade doctrine is entirely false. It wrongly assumes that even trade means no gain and that uneven trade creates winners and losers. Forced trade harms its supposed beneficiary, while free, natural trade always benefits both parties.
distilled: The balance-of-trade doctrine is built on false premises -- free trade always benefits both parties, though not always equally.
notes: Pivotal transition: from arguing within mercantilist premises to rejecting them outright. Smith's categorical claim: 'Nothing can be more absurd than this whole doctrine.' Two false premises demolished: (1) balanced trade = no gain, (2) imbalanced trade = one side loses. The positive-sum nature of voluntary trade -- one of Smith's most fundamental contributions. 'Always advantageous, though not always equally so, to both' -- nuanced: both gain, but not necessarily symmetrically. Cross-reference: 4.3.36-4.3.42 develop the positive-sum argument; 4.5.1 on bounties as a forced-trade mechanism.

refashioned:
By advantage or gain, I mean not the increase in the quantity of gold and silver, but the increase in the exchangeable value of the annual produce of a country's land and labor -- the increase in the annual revenue of its inhabitants.

If the balance of trade is even, and the trade between two places consists entirely of exchanging their own domestic products, both will usually not only gain, but gain equally or very nearly so. Each will provide a market for part of the other's surplus output. Each will replace a capital that had been employed in producing that surplus and had provided income and employment to a certain number of its inhabitants. Some of the inhabitants of each country will therefore derive their income and livelihood directly from the other.

Since the goods exchanged are assumed to be of equal value, the two capitals employed in the trade will usually be equal or nearly so. Both being employed in producing domestic goods, the income and employment they generate for each country's inhabitants will be equal or very nearly equal. This mutual income will be greater or smaller in proportion to the scale of their trade. If they do business worth 100,000 pounds each year, each country provides 100,000 pounds of annual income to the other's inhabitants. If a million pounds, then a million.
elegant: Gain means not more gold and silver but a higher exchangeable value of annual output -- greater national income. When balanced trade consists of exchanging domestic products, both countries gain equally: each provides a market for the other's surplus, replacing capital and generating income. With equal-value goods, equal capitals are employed, producing equal incomes. The mutual benefit scales with the volume of trade.
summary: True economic gain is increased national income, not accumulated gold. Balanced trade in domestic products benefits both countries equally by providing markets for each other's surplus output. The mutual benefit is proportional to the scale of trade.
distilled: Gain means higher national income, not more gold -- and balanced trade in domestic products benefits both sides equally, in proportion to its volume.
notes: Smith's redefinition of gain: not gold accumulation but increased 'exchangeable value of annual produce' -- i.e., national income / GDP. The mutual-gain theorem for balanced trade in domestic goods. Each country's trade replaces capital and sustains employment in the other -- international interdependence as a positive-sum game. The proportionality claim: benefits scale linearly with trade volume. Cross-reference: 0.0.1 on national wealth as annual produce; 4.3.37 on asymmetric trade.

refashioned:
If their trade were such that one country exported only domestic products while the other's returns consisted entirely of foreign goods, the balance would still be considered even -- commodities being paid for with commodities. Both would still gain, but not equally. The country exporting only domestic products would derive the greater revenue from the trade.

If England, for example, imported nothing from France but French domestic products, and -- having no goods France wanted -- paid for them annually by sending large quantities of foreign goods like tobacco and East India merchandise, this trade would generate some income for inhabitants of both countries but more for the French than the English. The entire French capital employed in the trade would circulate annually among the people of France. But only that part of English capital used to produce the English goods that originally purchased the foreign merchandise would circulate among the English. The greater part would replace capitals employed in Virginia, India, and China, providing income and employment to the inhabitants of those distant countries.

If the capitals were equal, this use of French capital would increase the revenue of the French people much more than the English capital would increase the revenue of the English people. France would be conducting a direct foreign trade of consumption with England, whereas England would be conducting a roundabout trade of the same kind with France. The different effects of direct and roundabout trade have already been fully explained.
elegant: Even when trade is balanced in value, gains can be unequal. If one country exports domestic products while the other pays with foreign goods (e.g., colonial tobacco, East India merchandise), the domestic-goods exporter gains more. Its entire trading capital circulates domestically, while the other country's capital mostly replaces investments in distant lands. Direct trade of consumption generates more domestic income than roundabout trade through third countries.
summary: Balanced trade can still produce unequal gains. The country exporting domestic products benefits more because its entire trading capital circulates domestically, while the country paying with foreign goods sees most of its capital sustaining employment in distant countries rather than at home.
distilled: The country exporting domestic products gains more than one paying with foreign goods, because direct trade generates more domestic income than roundabout trade through third countries.
notes: Asymmetric gains even in balanced trade -- the composition of exports matters. Direct trade of consumption vs. roundabout trade of consumption -- a key Book II distinction applied here. The Virginia-India-China example: English capital employed abroad generates foreign, not domestic, income. France gains more because 100% of its trading capital circulates domestically. Not an argument against the trade -- both still gain. Just an argument for the superiority of direct over roundabout trade. Cross-reference: Book II on direct vs. roundabout trade; 4.3.42 on the same point regarding gold/silver payments.

refashioned: In reality, there is probably no trade between any two countries that consists entirely of exchanging either domestic products on both sides, or domestic products on one side and foreign goods on the other. Almost all countries exchange a mix of domestic and foreign goods with each other. The country whose exports contain the greatest proportion of domestic goods and the smallest proportion of foreign goods will always be the principal gainer.
elegant: In practice, all trade involves a mix of domestic and foreign goods. The country whose exports are most heavily weighted toward domestic products will always gain the most from the exchange.
summary: Real trade always involves a mix of domestic and foreign goods. The country exporting the highest proportion of its own domestic products gains the most.
distilled: In practice, the country whose exports contain the highest share of domestic products gains the most from trade.
notes: A realistic qualifier -- pure cases are theoretical; real trade is mixed. The practical rule: maximize the domestic content of exports to maximize gains. This is not protectionism -- Smith is not recommending restricting imports, just noting that domestic production for export generates more domestic income. Cross-reference: 4.3.37 on the direct vs. roundabout trade distinction that underlies this principle.

refashioned: If England paid for its annual French imports not with tobacco and East India goods but with gold and silver, the balance would be considered uneven, since commodities would not be paid for with commodities but with precious metals. Yet this trade would still generate some income for inhabitants of both countries, though more for the French than the English. It would produce some income for the English because the capital employed in producing the English goods that originally purchased the gold and silver -- the capital that had been distributed among and provided income to certain English workers -- would thereby be replaced and enabled to continue that employment.
elegant: Even if England paid France with gold and silver instead of goods, both countries would still gain -- though France would gain more. England would still benefit because the capital used to produce the goods that originally earned that gold and silver would be replaced, sustaining English employment.
summary: Even trade paid in gold and silver rather than goods benefits both countries. England still gains because the capital that produced the goods originally exchanged for the precious metals is replaced, maintaining employment.
distilled: Paying for imports with gold and silver still benefits the paying country, because the capital that originally produced the goods exchanged for those metals is replaced.
notes: Directly challenges the mercantilist panic about gold outflows. Even the worst case scenario (paying in specie) still generates domestic benefits. The capital-replacement argument: gold exported was earned by domestic production, which sustains domestic employment regardless of what form the export takes. 'Commodities not being paid for with commodities' -- Smith uses the mercantilist framing to demolish it. Cross-reference: 4.3.40-4.3.41 develop this argument further.

refashioned: England's total capital would no more be diminished by exporting gold and silver than by exporting an equal value of any other goods. On the contrary, it would usually be augmented. No goods are sent abroad unless the demand for them is expected to be greater abroad than at home, and the returns are expected to be worth more at home than the goods exported. If tobacco worth only 100,000 pounds in England is sent to France and purchases wine worth 110,000 pounds in England, the exchange increases England's capital by 10,000 pounds.
elegant: Exporting gold and silver no more diminishes national capital than exporting any other goods -- in fact, trade usually increases it. Goods are only exported when they fetch more abroad than at home. If 100,000 pounds of tobacco buys 110,000 pounds worth of wine, England's capital grows by 10,000 pounds.
summary: Exporting gold and silver does not diminish national capital any more than exporting other goods. Trade typically increases capital because goods are exported to markets where they command higher prices, and the returns are worth more at home.
distilled: Exporting gold and silver does not diminish national capital -- trade increases it because exports fetch more abroad than they are worth at home.
notes: The gains-from-trade argument in its simplest form: export what is worth less at home, import what is worth more. Gold and silver are just another commodity -- no special status. The 10,000 pound gain illustrates the surplus from trade. 'No goods are sent abroad but those for which the demand is supposed to be greater abroad' -- the price-arbitrage basis of all trade. Cross-reference: 4.3.41 extends this to gold specifically; 4.1.1-4.1.3 on the mercantilist obsession with precious metals.

refashioned: If 100,000 pounds of English gold likewise purchases French wine worth 110,000 pounds in England, this exchange equally increases England's capital by 10,000 pounds. A merchant who has 110,000 pounds worth of wine in his cellar is a richer man than one who has only 100,000 pounds worth of tobacco in his warehouse -- and he is equally richer than one who has only 100,000 pounds worth of gold in his coffers. He can put a greater quantity of industry into motion and provide income, employment, and subsistence to a greater number of people than either of the other two.
elegant: If 100,000 pounds in gold buys wine worth 110,000 pounds at home, England's capital grows by 10,000 pounds -- exactly as with tobacco. A merchant with 110,000 pounds of wine is richer than one with 100,000 pounds of gold, because the wine puts more industry into motion and employs more people.
summary: Exporting gold for wine of greater value increases national capital just as any other profitable trade does. A merchant with 110,000 pounds of wine is wealthier than one with 100,000 pounds of gold, because the wine generates more employment and income.
distilled: Wine worth 110,000 pounds is more productive wealth than gold worth 100,000 pounds -- what matters is not what form wealth takes but how much industry it employs.
notes: The culmination of the anti-mercantilist argument: gold has no special status as wealth. The merchant comparison: wine > tobacco > gold in terms of productive capacity, because what matters is employing industry, not holding metal. 'Put into motion a greater quantity of industry' -- Smith's consistent measure of wealth is employment capacity, not bullion stock. Cross-reference: 4.1.1-4.1.3 on the popular equation of wealth with money; Book I on the real measure of value.

refashioned: But a country's capital equals the combined capital of all its inhabitants, and the quantity of industry that can be maintained annually equals what all those capitals together can sustain. Both the country's capital and its annual industry must therefore generally be increased by this exchange. It would indeed be more advantageous for England to purchase French wine with its own hardware and broadcloth than with either Virginia tobacco or Brazilian and Peruvian gold and silver. Direct foreign trade for consumption is always more advantageous than the roundabout kind.
elegant: A country's capital is the sum of its inhabitants' capital, so what increases individual capitals increases national capital. Profitable trade -- even in gold -- increases the nation's productive capacity. But direct trade (English hardware for French wine) is more advantageous than roundabout trade (via colonial tobacco or American gold), because it keeps more capital circulating domestically.
summary: Since national capital is the sum of individual capitals, profitable trade of any kind increases national wealth. Direct trade in domestic products is preferable to roundabout trade through colonial goods or precious metals, as it keeps more capital employed at home.
distilled: Profitable trade increases national capital regardless of what is traded, but direct exchange of domestic products is more advantageous than roundabout trade through colonial intermediaries.
notes: Aggregation argument: national capital = sum of individual capitals, so trade that enriches individuals enriches the nation. Hierarchy of trade forms: direct domestic-goods trade > roundabout via colonial goods > roundabout via gold/silver -- but all three are positive. This is a ranking of better and best, not good and bad. Cross-reference: Book II on the hierarchy of capital employment; 4.3.37 on direct vs. roundabout trade.

refashioned: But a roundabout trade of consumption carried on with gold and silver does not seem to be any less advantageous than any other equally roundabout trade. Nor is a country without mines more likely to be exhausted of gold and silver by annually exporting those metals than a country that does not grow tobacco is likely to be exhausted of tobacco by annually exporting it. Just as a country that has the means to buy tobacco will never long lack it, neither will a country that has the means to purchase gold and silver ever long lack those metals.
elegant: Roundabout trade in gold and silver is no worse than any other roundabout trade. A country without mines will no more run out of gold than a non-tobacco-growing country runs out of tobacco. Whatever can be purchased will be purchased -- precious metals are no exception.
summary: Trading in gold and silver is no more disadvantageous than trading in any other goods via roundabout routes. A country that can afford to buy gold and silver will always have them, just as a country that can buy tobacco will never lack it.
distilled: A country that can afford gold and silver will always have them -- precious metals flow to purchasing power just like any other commodity.
notes: The tobacco-gold analogy -- one of Smith's most effective rhetorical devices against mercantilism. Precious metals are commodities, not unique stores of national power. 'Has wherewithal to purchase' -- purchasing power, not mining, determines a nation's metal supply. This demolishes the mercantilist fear of gold exhaustion through trade. Cross-reference: 4.1.1-4.1.8 on the mercantilist obsession with accumulating precious metals.

refashioned: It is said to be a losing trade when a workman spends his earnings at the alehouse, and that the trade a manufacturing nation carries on with a wine country may be considered similar. I answer that the alehouse trade is not necessarily a losing one. In its own nature, it is just as advantageous as any other, though perhaps somewhat more liable to abuse. The work of a brewer, and even of a retail seller of fermented liquors, is as necessary a division of labor as any other. It will generally be more advantageous for a worker to buy beer from the brewer than to brew it himself, and if he is a poor worker, it will generally be cheaper to buy by the small amount from the retailer than in large quantities from the brewer. He may of course buy too much from either -- just as he may from any other dealer in his neighborhood: from the butcher if he is a glutton, or from the cloth dealer if he fancies being a dandy among his companions.
elegant: The claim that trading with a wine country is like a workman wasting wages at the alehouse is wrong. The alehouse trade is as legitimate as any other -- brewing and retailing are necessary divisions of labor. A worker benefits from buying beer rather than brewing it himself. He may buy too much, just as he may overindulge at the butcher's or clothier's -- but that is not a defect of the trade itself.
summary: The analogy between wine trade and a workman's alehouse spending is false. Brewing and retail are legitimate divisions of labor. Buying beer is rational; only excess consumption is the problem, and that risk exists with every trade, not just liquor.
distilled: Trade with wine countries is as legitimate as any other -- the risk of overconsumption exists with all goods, not just alcohol.
notes: Refutes the alehouse analogy used to discredit wine trade. Division of labor applied to brewing and retail -- even liquor trades are economically legitimate. The overconsumption risk is universal (butcher, clothier) -- not specific to alcohol. Smith refuses to let moral disapproval of drinking corrupt economic analysis. The 'beau among his companions' -- vanity in clothing is equivalent to excess in drink. Cross-reference: 4.3.45-4.3.47 extend the argument about cheap wine and sobriety.

refashioned: It is advantageous to the great body of workers that all these trades should be free, even though this freedom may be abused in all of them and is perhaps more likely to be abused in some than others. Though individuals may sometimes ruin their fortunes by excessive consumption of alcohol, there seems to be no risk that a nation will do so. In every country, while there are many who spend more on such liquors than they can afford, there are always far more who spend less. It also deserves notice that, if we consult experience, the cheapness of wine seems to cause not drunkenness but sobriety.
elegant: Freedom of trade benefits workers even if some abuse it -- some individuals may drink to ruin, but nations never do, since the overspenders are always outnumbered by the underspenders. Experience shows that cheap wine actually promotes sobriety rather than drunkenness.
summary: Free trade in alcohol benefits workers overall, even though some individuals may overconsume. Nations never drink themselves to ruin because the moderate always outnumber the excessive. Experience shows that cheap wine promotes sobriety, not drunkenness.
distilled: Free trade in alcohol benefits the majority -- and experience shows that cheap wine promotes sobriety, not drunkenness.
notes: Individual vs. aggregate argument: some individuals overindulge, but the nation as a whole does not. The 'cheap wine = sobriety' thesis -- counterintuitive and empirical. Smith appeals to experience over moralizing. Foreshadows 4.3.46-4.3.47 with specific evidence from wine-producing countries. Free trade even in 'vice goods' is Smith's position -- a strong libertarian stance for the 18th century. Cross-reference: 4.3.46 on wine country sobriety; modern debates over alcohol pricing policy.

refashioned: The inhabitants of wine-producing countries are generally the most sober people in Europe -- witness the Spaniards, the Italians, and the inhabitants of southern France. People are seldom guilty of excess in what they consume every day. Nobody tries to display liberality and good fellowship by being lavish with a drink as cheap as small beer. On the contrary, in countries that produce no grapes -- whether from excessive heat or cold -- where wine is consequently expensive and rare, drunkenness is a common vice. This is true of the northern nations and of all those living in the tropics, such as the peoples along the coast of Guinea.
elegant: Wine-producing countries -- Spain, Italy, southern France -- are the most sober in Europe. People rarely overindulge in their daily fare, and no one shows off by being generous with a cheap drink. In countries without vineyards, where wine is expensive and rare, drunkenness is far more common -- as seen in northern Europe and the tropics.
summary: Wine-producing countries like Spain, Italy, and southern France are Europe's most sober. Where wine is a cheap daily commodity, excess is rare. Drunkenness is most common where wine is scarce and expensive -- in northern Europe and the tropics.
distilled: Wine-producing countries are the most sober because people do not overindulge in what is cheap and commonplace -- drunkenness flourishes where wine is scarce and expensive.
notes: Empirical evidence for the cheap-wine-sobriety thesis. Spain, Italy, southern France as exemplars of temperate drinking cultures. The psychological mechanism: cheap daily fare does not inspire conspicuous consumption or social display. The inverse: scarcity and expense make wine a status drink, encouraging binge consumption. Cross-reference: 4.3.47 on the French regiment example; modern evidence on the 'availability paradox' in alcohol consumption research.

refashioned:
When a French regiment is transferred from the northern provinces of France, where wine is somewhat expensive, to be quartered in the south, where it is very cheap, the soldiers -- I have frequently heard it observed -- are at first led astray by the cheapness and novelty of good wine. But after a few months in residence, most of them become as sober as the rest of the inhabitants.

If the duties on foreign wines and the excise taxes on malt, beer, and ale were all removed at once, it might similarly cause a fairly widespread temporary bout of drunkenness in Great Britain among the middle and lower classes, which would probably soon be followed by a permanent and nearly universal sobriety. Drunkenness is currently by no means the vice of fashionable people or those who can easily afford the most expensive liquors. A gentleman drunk on ale has scarcely ever been seen among us.
elegant: French soldiers transferred from the expensive north to the cheap south initially overindulge in wine, but within months become as sober as locals. Abolishing wine and beer duties in Britain would likely cause a brief epidemic of drunkenness among the middle and lower classes, followed by permanent sobriety. Drunkenness is already not a vice of the wealthy, who can afford the best -- a gentleman drunk on ale is virtually unknown.
summary: French soldiers transferred to cheap wine country initially overindulge but quickly become sober. Similarly, abolishing alcohol duties in Britain would cause brief excess followed by lasting sobriety. The wealthy, who already have access to fine liquor, are not the ones who drink to excess.
distilled: The French regiment example proves that initial overindulgence from cheap wine is temporary -- lasting sobriety follows as the novelty wears off.
notes: The French regiment as a natural experiment in alcohol pricing effects. The adjustment period: initial excess followed by convergence to local norms. Policy implication: abolish alcohol duties for long-term sobriety gains despite short-term excess. Class observation: drunkenness is a vice of those who cannot easily afford drink, not of those who can. 'A gentleman drunk with ale' -- Smith uses social observation as evidence. Cross-reference: 4.3.45-4.3.46 on the cheap wine thesis; modern parallels in alcohol policy research.

refashioned:
Britain's wine trade restrictions, moreover, do not seem designed to keep people from the alehouse so much as to keep them from the alehouse where the best and cheapest liquor is sold. They favor Portuguese wine and discourage French. The Portuguese, it is said, are better customers for our manufactures than the French, and should therefore be preferred. Since they give us their custom, we should give them ours.

The petty tactics of small-time shopkeepers are thus elevated into political maxims for governing a great empire. It is only the most petty tradesmen who make it a rule to patronize mainly their own customers. A great merchant buys his goods wherever they are cheapest and best, without regard to such narrow considerations.
elegant: Britain's wine restrictions do not promote temperance -- they just steer consumers from cheap, good French wine to dearer Portuguese. The justification -- that Portugal buys more British manufactures -- elevates petty shopkeeper logic into imperial policy. A great merchant buys wherever goods are cheapest and best, without regard to who reciprocates his custom.
summary: British wine policy does not promote sobriety but merely redirects consumption from cheaper French wine to more expensive Portuguese. The logic that nations should buy from whoever buys from them is the reasoning of petty shopkeepers, not the wisdom appropriate to governing an empire.
distilled: Britain's wine restrictions do not promote temperance but merely favor Portuguese over French wine based on petty reciprocal-purchasing logic unworthy of a great trading nation.
notes: The Methuen Treaty (1703) context -- preferential duties favoring Portuguese over French wine. 'Sneaking arts of underling tradesmen' -- one of Smith's most contemptuous phrases for mercantilist reasoning. The great trader vs. the petty shopkeeper -- a metaphor for sound vs. unsound trade policy. Reciprocal purchasing as a fallacy: buy where quality and price are best, not where customers are. Cross-reference: 4.3.49 on the broader critique of beggar-thy-neighbor policies.

refashioned:
By maxims like these, nations have been taught that their interest lies in beggaring all their neighbors. Each nation has been made to view the prosperity of every nation it trades with as its own loss, and to regard their gain with envious eyes. Commerce, which ought naturally to be a bond of union and friendship among nations as among individuals, has become the most fertile source of discord and animosity.

The capricious ambition of kings and ministers has not, during this and the preceding century, been more fatal to the peace of Europe than the impertinent jealousy of merchants and manufacturers. The violence and injustice of rulers is an ancient evil for which, I am afraid, human nature can scarcely provide a remedy. But the mean greed and monopolizing spirit of merchants and manufacturers -- who neither are nor ought to be the rulers of mankind -- though it cannot perhaps be corrected, can very easily be prevented from disturbing the peace of anyone but themselves.
elegant: Mercantilist maxims taught nations to see neighbors' prosperity as their own loss, turning commerce -- which should be a bond of friendship -- into the greatest source of international conflict. The jealousy of merchants and manufacturers has been as destructive to European peace as the ambition of kings. Royal violence is an ancient, perhaps incurable evil. But the monopolizing greed of merchants, who are not and should not be mankind's rulers, can at least be prevented from disturbing everyone else's peace.
summary: Mercantilism taught nations to view trade as zero-sum, turning commerce from a bond of friendship into a source of war. The jealousy of merchants and manufacturers has been as destructive to European peace as royal ambition, though unlike royal tyranny, mercantile greed can be contained.
distilled: Mercantilism turned commerce from a bond of friendship into a source of war by teaching nations to see their neighbors' prosperity as their own loss.
notes: One of the most powerful passages in the Wealth of Nations. Commerce as natural bond of friendship vs. mercantilism as source of war. Two sources of European conflict: (1) royal ambition (ancient, incurable), (2) mercantile jealousy (modern, containable). 'Neither are, nor ought to be, the rulers of mankind' -- Smith's democratic conviction that merchants should not dictate policy. 'Mean rapacity, the monopolizing spirit' -- Smith at his most vehement. Cross-reference: 4.2.45 on manufacturers intimidating the legislature; 4.3.58 on Anglo-French animosity specifically.
